title: Freedom of speech
description: Test your skills in this exercise on the First Amendment's protection of free speech.
topic_path: The First Amendment: freedom of speech | 3.3

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous rulings, the Supreme Court is most likely to view a case concerning which of the following as a symbolic speech case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A man who was arrested for waving a Nazi flag at a rally
A newspaper editor who was sued for defamation after printing an article criticizing a candidate for Senate
A bookstore owner who was sued for displaying obscene magazines in an area where children could see them
A member of the Ku Klux Klan is arrested for distributing pamphlets advocating racial discrimination

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous rulings, which of the following scenarios would most likely violate the freedom of speech clause of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A man is charged with a crime for displaying an American flag with a taped peace symbol on it
A teenager is arrested for shouting “Bomb!” in an airport and pointing to his friend’s bag, causing people to panic
A woman is arrested for yelling death threats at a group of children as they are walking to school
A public school district requires students to recite a non-denominational prayer at the start of the school day

assessment_items.question_content:
>"The school officials banned and sought to punish petitioners for a silent, passive expression of opinion, unaccompanied by any disorder or disturbance on the part of petitioners. There is here no evidence whatever of petitioners' interference, actual or nascent, with the schools' work or of collision with the rights of other students to be secure and to be let alone. Accordingly, this case does not concern speech or action that intrudes upon the work of the schools or the rights of other students."

-Justice Abe Fortas

**Which of the following Supreme Court cases is most relevant to the topic of the passage? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Tinker v. Des Moines* (1969)
*Schenck v. United States* (1919)
*New York Times Co. v. United States* (1971)
*Engel v. Vitale* (1962)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous Supreme Court rulings, which act of free speech would most likely be protected as symbolic speech under the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A protester is arrested for burning a U.S. flag at a political rally.
A student is suspended for yelling at a teacher during class.
A man is arrested for handing out leaflets that call for immediate violence toward a public official.
A woman is fined for playing loud music after midnight to protest a local noise regulation.

assessment_items.question_content:
To express opposition to a recent military action by the U.S. government, a high school student wears a black t-shirt with a peace sign on it to school. The shirt doesn't contain profanity or threats, but school officials say it's disruptive and send the student home. The student’s parents sue the school district.

**Which principle is most relevant to the legal argument the student’s family is likely to make?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Students do not lose all rights to express political views in school.
The government cannot restrict religious expression in public schools.
A man is arrested for handing out leaflets that call for immediate violence toward a public official.
The federal government cannot censor the press without due process.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Political cartoon depicting Uncle Sam dragging figures identified as traitors and labor organizers away from the Capitol Building, which is flying a flag reading 'Sedition Law Passed.'](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5938a3505b02ed1387afb46935f99e1c.jpg)
*W.A Rogers, "Now for a round-up," 1918.* Cartoon depicting Uncle Sam rounding up men labeled "Spy" and "Traitor," while a flag reading "Sedition Law Passed" flies over the US Capitol. Source: [Library of Congress](http://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2010717793/)

**Which of the following Supreme Court cases is most relevant to the topic of the cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Schenck v. United States* (1919)
*Tinker v. Des Moines* (1969)
*New York Times Co. v. United States* (1971)
*Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous rulings, which of the following scenarios would most likely violate the freedom of speech clause of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A public school district bans students from wearing t-shirts supporting presidential candidates before an election
A woman is arrested for shouting “Fire!” in a theater when there was no real emergency, causing a panic
A state law prevents students from reading religious texts in public schools
A city ordinance requires a protest movement to obtain a permit before conducting a peaceful march

assessment_items.question_content:
In 1984, Gregory Lee Johnson burned an American flag in front of the Dallas City Hall in protest to Reagan administration policies. He was tried and convicted under a Texas law that prohibited people from desecrating a respected object. The case eventually went to the Supreme Court, which  ruled in a 5-4 decision that Johnson had not violated the Constitution. 

**Which of the following constitutional provisions does the case described in the scenario have in common with *Tinker v. Des Moines* (1969)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Freedom of speech clause
Assembly and petition clause
Due process clause
Free exercise clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the two court cases?**

 | *Schenck v. United States* (1919) | *Tinker v. Des Moines* (1969)
- | - | -
**A** | Political spending is a form of protected speech  | The right of privacy extends to a woman’s decision to have an abortion
**B** | Congress may not use the commerce clause to make possession of a gun in a school zone a federal crime  | School sponsorship of religious activities violates the establishment clause 
**C** | The US government cannot block the publication of secret government documents | Compelling Amish students to attend school past the eighth grade violates the free exercise clause 
**D** | Speech creating a “clear and present danger” is not protected by the First Amendment | Public school students have the right to wear black armbands to protest the Vietnam War

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the two court cases?**

 | *Schenck v. United States* (1919) | *Tinker v. Des Moines* (1969)
- | - | -
**A** | Ruled that the federal government had violated the due process clause of the Fifth Amendment  | Ruled that the state had violated the freedom of speech clause of the First Amendment
**B** | Applied the First Amendment to the states  | Decided that the Supreme Court can  declare a law unconstitutional 
**C** | Declared that the federal government can restrict certain types of speech if the words create a “clear and present danger” | Declared that wearing a black armband to protest the Vietnam War was a form of symbolic speech 
**D** | Led to an increase in power for the national government to restrict freedom of speech | Led to an increase in state power to restrict freedom of speech

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The character of every act depends upon the circumstances in which it is done. The most stringent protection of free speech would not protect a man in falsely shouting fire in a theatre and causing a panic.”

>Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. 
 

**Supporters of Justice Holmes’s argument could point to which of the following cases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Schenck v. United States* (1919)
*Shaw v. Reno* (1993)
*Marbury v. Madison*  (1803)
*Gideon v. Wainwright* (1963)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of how a state can restrict protected speech under the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: New York passes a law banning groups from staging protests in major highways during rush hour traffic
California passes a law prohibiting any person from organizing a rally that advocates for the end of police violence
Texas passes a law that fines teachers who protest pay cuts during school hours
West Virginia passes a law that restricts students from wearing Trump Campaign memorabilia at televised events at public universities

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of how a state can reasonably restrict speech protected under the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A group of protesters is arrested after staging a sit-in on the governor’s front lawn to protest a Voter ID law
A group of workers is arrested for picketing on the sidewalk outside a restaurant during the day, claiming that they are paid less than the minimum wage
A man is arrested after organizing a protest against a new sanctuary city law proposed by his state legislature
A woman is arrested for wearing a shirt that says “End military involvement in the Middle East”

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of how the federal government can restrict protected speech under the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) makes a rule requiring television networks to use an automated beep when a vulgar word is said on broadcast television
Congress passes a law banning the sale of violent games to children under the age of 12
Congress passes a law that criminalizes depictions of a person using drugs like marijuana
The Federal Election Commission (FEC) makes a rule banning newspapers from publishing political cartoons that negatively depict political officials

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which scenario best represents a legal restriction on speech under the time, place, and manner doctrine?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A city regulation prevents protests on highways during rush hour.
A state law bans protest signs that criticize government officials.
A public school suspends a student for writing a newspaper editorial opposing a war.
A state fines people who express anti-police views at public hearings.
